title: Compatibility of Canonical Local Systems and F-isocrystals on Shimura Varieties
speaker: Stefan Patrikis
abstract: I will discuss compatibility of the canonical G-valued l-adic local systems on Shimura varieties of non-abelian type G. In previous work with Klevdal, we settled this for the projections of the local systems to the adjoint group of G, and this talk will focus on lifting the compatibility to G using work of Bakker-Shankar-Tsimerman. I will also discuss the corresponding F-isocrystal compatibility statements, resulting from joint work with Huryn, Kedlaya, and Klevdal.

proof_stages.label: Deligne-style compatibility as the target
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from Deligne’s l-independence theorem and its crystalline analogue for smooth projective varieties over finite fields, setting the desired model of Frobenius compatibility.

proof_stages.label: Canonical local systems from the Shimura tower
proof_stages.summary: For a Shimura datum, the speaker recalls the canonical tower, special-point rationality, then restricts to a geometrically connected finite-level component S to obtain G(Q_l)-valued fundamental-group representations rho_l.

proof_stages.label: G-valued compatibility theorem
proof_stages.summary: Under the real-rank/superrigidity hypotheses, the main theorem is formulated: the rho_l spread to a common integral model, their Frobenius semisimple classes lie in G//G over Q independently of l, and matching G-valued overconvergent F-isocrystals give the crystalline comparison.

proof_stages.label: Scope beyond abelian type and the full-G issue
proof_stages.summary: The theorem is situated relative to known abelian-type results and to earlier adjoint-group results, and central-cover lifting phenomena are used to explain why passing from G^ad to G is not formal.

proof_stages.label: Uniform spread-out and ramification control
proof_stages.summary: The first assertion is obtained by spreading compactified automorphic data over a localization and by an abstract integrality argument using superrigidity together with the theory of companions.

proof_stages.label: Adjoint compatibility and μ-ordinary canonical lifts
proof_stages.summary: Previously proved G^ad-valued compatibility is combined with Bakker-Shankar-Tsimerman’s integral canonical model results to produce special canonical lifts of μ-ordinary points.

proof_stages.label: Companion-and-monodromy upgrade to full l-adic compatibility
proof_stages.summary: Using the μ-ordinary special lifts, l-to-l' companions over finite fields, compatibility at special points, and a big-monodromy argument, the adjoint/special-point information is promoted to the full G-valued l-independence statement.

proof_stages.label: Crystalline comparison via overconvergent G-F-isocrystals
proof_stages.summary: For the p-adic assertion one constructs G-valued overconvergent F-isocrystals with controlled monodromy, using p-adic Riemann-Hilbert comparison, and then applies the same companions-and-monodromy strategy to match crystalline and l-adic Frobenius classes.

examples.name: Siegel modular variety and Tate modules
examples.note: The Siegel case G=GSp_{2g}, where the Shimura variety parametrizes principally polarized abelian varieties and the local systems are l-adic Tate modules, illustrates the familiar moduli-theoretic situation in which compatibility is classical.
examples.engagement_reason: The Siegel case is used as a familiar modular-interpretation example where the local systems are Tate modules, with no concrete computation or verification carried out.

passes.reason: The Siegel/GSp Shimura variety with abelian-variety Tate modules is the standard moduli case used as a positive benchmark for compatibility.
The Siegel modular variety with its abelian-variety Tate modules is the standard modular-interpretable case used as a positive motivating example.

Used as the standard Siegel-modular/Tate-module case where compatibility is already known, with no structurally exceptional feature beyond being classical.
Used as the standard moduli/abelian-type case where the local systems are ordinary Tate modules and compatibility is already known.
Used as the standard abelian-type/moduli case where the local systems are ordinary Tate modules and ℓ-independence is known.

examples.name: Kuga-Satake lift of K3 cohomology
examples.note: The Kuga-Satake construction is cited as a known hard instance of lifting an orthogonal compatible system through a central cover to GSpin, illustrating the general lifting problem rather than driving the proof here.
examples.engagement_reason: The Kuga-Satake construction is cited as an example of a lift from K3 primitive cohomology to GSpin coming from an abelian variety, but the construction is not run in detail.

passes.reason: The Kuga-Satake lift of K3 primitive cohomology is a standard infinite-family construction illustrating a positive lift, not a failure.
The Kuga-Satake construction for K3 cohomology is used as a known geometric lifting example, not as a failure case, and K3/GSpin data are not sporadic exceptions.
the Kuga-Satake lift is a systematic construction for K3-type orthogonal cohomology, used as a positive lifting example.
The Kuga–Satake/GSpin lift of K3 primitive cohomology is a standard systematic construction used as a positive example of lifting.
Kuga-Satake for K3 cohomology is a standard spin-lift construction in a family, cited as a successful lift rather than an obstruction
the Kuga-Satake construction is a systematic lift of K3 primitive cohomology to a GSpin/abelian-variety realization, used as a positive example of lifting
Cited as a systematic positive Kuga-Satake lift of K3 primitive cohomology through GSpin, not as a failure or sporadic object.
Cited as a positive instance of a compatible lift through GSpin via the Kuga-Satake construction, and K3/Kuga-Satake objects occur in systematic families.
Used as a positive example of a motivic GSpin lift of K3 primitive cohomology, not as a failure case or sporadic object.
Used as a positive illustration of a central GSpin lift of K3 cohomology via the systematic Kuga-Satake construction.
The Kuga–Satake construction is cited as a positive example where a K3 cohomology representation lifts to a spin/GSpin representation from an abelian variety.
Used as a positive example of a central lift supplied by the Kuga-Satake construction, not as a failure, and K3/Kuga-Satake objects occur in infinite families.

examples.name: Type-D Mumford-Tate abelian variety and non-abelian-type lift
examples.note: An abelian variety with a type-D Mumford-Tate group admits geometric lifts to a simply connected cover that cannot come from the Tannakian category generated by H^1 of abelian varieties, illustrating why full G-valued compatibility is genuinely stronger than adjoint or abelian-type results.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker sets up the type-D abelian-variety case, chooses a covering group, traces the resulting geometric lifts, and explains their non-abelian-type/Tannakian consequence.

passes.reason: The type-D Mumford-Tate example is an infinite-family obstruction showing a geometric lift need not come from the Tannakian category generated by abelian-variety H1.
The type-D Mumford-Tate example is used to show that a geometric lift need not lie in the Tannakian category generated by abelian-variety H^1, so abelian-type methods do not suffice.
the type-D lift belongs to a classical family but is used to show that such lifts need not come from the abelian-variety Tannakian category.
The type-D lift is used to show that such geometric lifts need not lie in the Tannakian category generated by H^1 of abelian varieties.
the type-D lift is from a classical infinite family but is used to show such lifts need not come from the abelian-variety H^1 Tannakian category
the type-D lift is used to show that a geometric lift can fail to come from the Tannakian category generated by abelian varieties
Used to show that a type-D lift can fail to come from the Tannakian category generated by abelian-variety H^1s, while type D is a systematic infinite family.
Used to show that a natural type-D lift cannot come from the Tannakian category generated by abelian-variety H1s, with the lift lying at the simply-connected end of the isogeny tower.
Deployed to show that a type-D geometric lift need not come from the Tannakian category generated by abelian-variety H^1, though type D is a classical family.
Used to show that a geometric type-D lift can fail to lie in the Tannakian category generated by H^1 of abelian varieties, while type D is a classical infinite family.
The type-D Mumford–Tate lift is used to show that such lifted representations need not come from the Tannakian category generated by abelian varieties, obstructing an abelian-type argument.
Used to show that the type-D lifted representation cannot come from the Tannakian category generated by abelian-variety H^1's, while type D is a classical infinite family.

examples.name: Esnault-Groechenig rigid-local-system integrality argument
examples.note: The proof of integrality for cohomologically rigid local systems supplies the pattern of specializing mod p, using companions, and unspecializing, which the speaker says is adapted to obtain uniform ramification control.
examples.engagement_reason: The Esnault-Groechenig integrality proof is unpacked step by step via bounded rigid systems, specialization, companions, and transfer of integrality.

passes.reason: Cohomologically rigid local systems are isolated minimal-deformation objects and the Esnault-Groechenig argument is cited as a proof template rather than a refutation.
Cohomologically rigid local systems are isolated zero-deformation objects in their moduli and are cited as a proof technique for integrality rather than as counterexamples.
cohomologically rigid local systems are isolated no-deformation points in their moduli, and the argument is cited as a positive proof template.
Cohomologically rigid local systems are isolated/no-deformation cases, and the integrality argument is cited as a methodological positive precedent.

Invoked as a proof strategy for integrality via cohomologically rigid local systems, whose rigidity makes them isolated/extremal points in their moduli.
Cohomologically rigid local systems are isolated no-deformation points in their moduli and are cited as a proof template for integrality.
Used as a methodological precedent for integrality via companions, and the local systems are cohomologically rigid, i.e. isolated/minimal-deformation objects.
